symbols: six wings — the defining attribute of seraphim from Isaiah 6: two covering the face (reverence before God's holiness), two covering the feet (humility), two for flying (readiness to serve)
golden background — the uncreated divine light, the glory of God's throne room
three seraphim descending vertically — the heavenly hierarchy in dynamic worship, the ceaseless attendance upon God
varied wing colors (white, blue, green, orange-red) — the multiform beauty of God's creation, the living fire of divine love
description: Three angelic figures with six wings each are arranged vertically against a golden-ochre background. The topmost figure has lavender and white wings spread wide, with a youthful face gazing downward. The middle figure has white and green-tinted wings outstretched, with two wings covering its body and a face looking forward. The lowest and largest figure has striking orange-red and golden wings, its face tilted upward with an expression of wonder. Each figure has a human-like face and head but their bodies are entirely enveloped by their multiple wings.
theological_description: The seraphim appear only in Isaiah 6:1-7, attending the throne of God and crying 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts.' Their six wings correspond to the threefold posture of worship: reverence (covering face before unbearable holiness), humility (covering feet, a euphemism for the body's lowliness before God), and service (flying to do God's will). The Trisagion they sing becomes foundational to Christian liturgy — the Sanctus of the Eucharist. Church Fathers like Pseudo-Dionysius placed seraphim at the summit of the celestial hierarchy, closest to God, burning with the fire of divine love (their name means 'burning ones'). The vertical arrangement suggests the cascade of divine glory from the throne downward through the heavenly court. Vasnetsov's cathedral art captures these beings as Isaiah experienced them — overwhelming, fiery, and wholly devoted to the holiness of God.

scripture_refs.reason: Isaiah's throne room vision where seraphim with six wings cry 'Holy, holy, holy'
The four living creatures with six wings ceaselessly praising God, the Johannine parallel to Isaiah's seraphim
Ezekiel's living creatures with multiple wings attending the divine throne, a related theophany
